1: Moses was tending the flock of Jethro his father-in-law, the priest of Midian — the same man called Reuel in Exodus 2:18 (Reuel as personal name, Jethro as title or second name meaning 'his excellence/abundance'). The back of the desert (achar hammidbar) — the far side, the uninhabited interior. Moses leads the flock to Horeb, the mountain of God. Horeb (= Sinai) is the narrator's prospective identification; Moses does not know the mountain's name yet. The traditional location is Jebel Musa in the southern Sinai Peninsula (ca. 2,285m), though an alternative tradition places it in the Hejaz in northwestern Saudi Arabia (Jebel al-Lawz). What the text establishes is the setting: the far wilderness, away from human settlement, where only isolation and silence allow divine encounter.
2: The angel of YHWH (malach YHWH) appeared to him in a flame of fire from within the bush. The bush is sneh — a thorny shrub, likely a type of acacia or bramble common in the Sinai desert. It burns but is not consumed. Fire is the consistent OT marker of divine presence: the pillar of fire (Ex Exodus 13:21), the fire on Sinai (Ex Exodus 19:18), the fire that consumed Elijah's offering (1 Kings 18:38), the coal from the altar at Isaiah's commissioning (Isaiah 6:6-7). The non-consuming fire distinguishes divine fire from ordinary destruction: holiness burns without consuming, illuminates without destroying — precisely the characteristic that will define the divine relationship with Israel.
3: 'I will turn aside and see this great sight — why the bush is not burned up.' Moses is a trained observer: a former Egyptian administrator and a shepherd, both roles requiring attention to anomalies. His curiosity is the prerequisite for encounter. The bush does not compel; it invites. The divine communication requires a willing turn, a decision to investigate. This structure — the divine initiative that awaits human response — is characteristic of YHWH's mode of encounter throughout the OT (cf. Elijah at the still small voice in 1 Kings 19:12-13).
4: 'Moses, Moses' — the doubled name signals divine urgency and personal intimacy, a rhetorical pattern shared with Abraham at the Aqedah (Genesis 22:11: 'Abraham, Abraham'), Samuel's first call (1 Samuel 3:10: 'Samuel, Samuel'), and later Paul on the Damascus road (Acts 9:4: 'Saul, Saul'). The double naming distinguishes this call from ordinary speech. 'Here I am' (hineni) — the response of complete availability, the same word Abraham uses at the Aqedah, Isaiah at his commissioning (Isaiah 6:8). The response before the command: readiness declared before the demand is made.
5: 'Take off your sandals, for the place where you are standing is holy ground (admat qodesh).' The removal of sandals signals that the wearer stands on ground that belongs to another — an act of submission and vulnerability (bare feet on sacred soil). The ground is holy not by nature but by presence: YHWH's presence sanctifies it. This principle runs throughout the tabernacle legislation and into temple theology. The same instruction is given to Joshua at Jericho (Joshua 5:15) — both commissions begin with the removal of sandals before the divine commander. The practice of removing shoes before holy spaces is preserved in Jewish, Islamic, and some Christian traditions.
6: 'I am the God of your father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.' The self-identification connects the Mosaic commission directly to the patriarchal covenant. Moses' generation had not experienced YHWH directly — the divine name had not been made known in its fullness (Ex 6:3 implies the patriarchs knew God as El Shaddai, not YHWH). The introduction grounds the new commission in the old covenant: this is not a new god but the God of the fathers. Moses hides his face — a standard response to theophany throughout the OT (cf. Isaiah 6:2 — the seraphim cover their faces; Ex Exodus 33:20 — no one can see YHWH's face and live). Jesus cites this verse in Mark 12:26-27 to argue for resurrection: the present-tense claim — 'I am the God of Abraham' — implies Abraham is presently alive before God.
7: 'I have surely seen the affliction of my people in Egypt, and have heard their cry because of their taskmasters. I know their sufferings.' The four verbs of divine attention from Exodus 2:24-25 are now given as direct divine speech. Yad'ati (I know) — the covenantal knowing of 2:25 becomes a spoken commitment. The three-fold statement (seen, heard, know) is the divine's formal acknowledgment of Israel's legal complaint. In ANE thought, when a suzerain acknowledges a vassal's appeal, it obligates the suzerain to act. YHWH's acknowledgment of Israel's cry is the basis for the mission that follows: I have seen, therefore I am sending.
8: 'I have come down to deliver them from the hand of the Egyptians and to bring them up out of that land to a good and broad land, a land flowing with milk and honey.' The divine descent to deliver — vareed lehatzilo — is incarnational language: the transcendent God comes down to act in history. 'Milk and honey' is a formulaic description of agricultural and pastoral abundance: milk from flocks (pastoral economy), honey from bees feeding on flowering vegetation (agricultural ecosystem). The six nations listed — Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites — are the standard pre-conquest inventory found throughout Deuteronomy and Joshua, a formulaic list that the Israelite audience would have recognized as the catalog of Canaan's inhabitants.
9: The cry of the Israelites has reached YHWH — the legal appeal of 2:23 is formally received at the divine court. The vocabulary is judicial: the oppression (lachatz = pressing down, oppression) of the Egyptians is presented as the grounds for a legal finding in Israel's favor. The divine judge has heard the appeal; the remedy is deliverance. This judicial framework for the Exodus is foundational to Israel's understanding of YHWH as judge and advocate of the powerless — a theme that runs through the Psalms, the prophets, and into the NT language of justification.
10: 'Come now, I will send you to Pharaoh that you may bring my people, the children of Israel, out of Egypt.' The commissioning formula. The word shalacha (I will send) is the verb from which shaliach (sent one, emissary, apostle) derives. Moses is not a volunteer but a shaliach — sent with authority, speaking in the name of the one who sent him. The NT apostolate (Greek apostolos = sent one) operates on this same legal principle: the one sent speaks with the authority of the sender and is to be received as the sender himself (cf. John 13:20: 'whoever receives the one I send receives me').
11: 'Who am I that I should go to Pharaoh and bring the children of Israel out of Egypt?' — Moses' first objection. The question is not false modesty but accurate self-assessment: he is a fugitive, an ex-administrator with no current standing, unknown to the current Pharaoh and distrusted by the Hebrews (Exodus 2:14). The divine answer to the identity question is not a résumé but a promise: 'I will be with you (ehyeh imach).' The commission rests not on Moses' qualifications but on divine accompaniment. This pattern — God choosing the unlikely and promising his presence — recurs with Gideon (Judges 6:12-16), Jeremiah (Jeremiah 1:6-8), and Paul (2 Corinthians 12:9-10).
12: 'This shall be the sign for you' — the sign is paradoxical: when you bring the people out, you will serve God on this mountain. Most biblical signs precede the action they confirm; this sign follows it. Moses cannot verify the sign before he acts — only after the mission succeeds will the sign become visible. This structure demands faith: trust the promise before the evidence arrives. The mountain is the same ground where Moses now stands; the pilgrimage to Sinai after the exodus is therefore already embedded in the commission. Sinai is not an accidental destination but the pre-announced goal of the exodus itself.
13: Moses' second objection: What is his name? The question anticipates the community's question — by whose authority do you come? In the ancient world, the name of a deity was not a label but a compressed disclosure of character, power, and covenant relationship. To act in the name of a god was to invoke that god's authority and stake the mission on that god's reputation. Moses needs more than 'the God of your fathers' — he needs a name that can be invoked in Pharaoh's court and believed by enslaved Israelites. The divine response to this request is the most important theological moment in the Torah.
14: 'Ehyeh asher ehyeh' — 'I AM WHO I AM' or 'I WILL BE WHAT I WILL BE.' The divine name is derived from the Hebrew root hayah (to be, to become, to be present). The form is imperfect — ongoing, unfinished — which gives it both an ontological meaning (I am absolute, unconditioned being) and a promissory meaning (I will be present in every future moment of need). The name cannot be fully translated because it asserts something about God that no human language was designed to express: self-existence, the ground of all being, the one who depends on nothing outside himself for his existence. This is the theological center of the OT doctrine of God, and the 'I am' statements of John's Gospel (ego eimi) are deliberate invocations of this divine name.
15: YHWH — the sacred Tetragrammaton, the covenant name of God. 'This is my name forever, and this is my memorial-name throughout all generations.' The name YHWH is proprietary to Israel's covenant relationship — it is not the generic divine name (Elohim, El) used across the ancient world, but the personal name by which Israel's God distinguishes himself. Second Temple Jews developed the practice of not pronouncing the Tetragrammaton, substituting Adonai (Lord) in reading — a practice reflected in most English Bible translations where YHWH appears as LORD. The name's revelation to Moses at Sinai is the moment Israel's distinctive covenant knowledge of God begins.
16: The elders of Israel (zeqenim — the elders) are the tribal leaders and heads of households who constituted Israel's pre-monarchic governance structure. During the Egyptian oppression, when there was no king and no formal government, the elders functioned as the representative body of the people. Moses is instructed to convene this body — his mission requires community authorization, not just divine commission. The assembly of elders is the legitimate political process through which the Exodus initiative will move from divine command to community action.
17: 'I will bring you up out of the affliction of Egypt, to the land of the Canaanites...' — the promise recapitulates the Abrahamic covenant (Genesis 15:18-21) and the Sinai promise of Exodus 3:8. The six nations are listed again (Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites) — the same catalog as Exodus 3:8. The formulaic repetition is deliberate: the elders of Israel need to hear the exact promise God made so they can recognize its fulfillment. The covenant vocabulary ('I promised') formally connects this moment to the patriarchal promises Moses is sent to fulfill.
18: The request to Pharaoh: 'Let us go a three-day journey into the wilderness to sacrifice to YHWH our God.' The initial demand is for a temporary departure for religious observance — not permanent exodus. Whether this represents a genuine first step in a negotiation (with full freedom as the ultimate goal) or a modest opening bid, it serves the narrative function of establishing that Pharaoh's refusal is not a response to an unreasonable demand. Even the minimum request (a three-day pilgrimage festival) will be refused, making Pharaoh's intransigence the basis for the escalating plagues.
19: YHWH predicts that Pharaoh will not let Israel go 'except by a mighty hand (yad chazaqah).' The strong hand of YHWH — yad chazaqah — becomes the signature phrase of the Exodus event, appearing over twenty times in Exodus and Deuteronomy. The Shema context in Deuteronomy 6:20-21 makes the strong hand the answer to the child's question: 'What do these statutes mean?' — 'We were slaves in Egypt and YHWH brought us out with a strong hand.' The phrase becomes the central confession of Israel's delivered identity: who we are is defined by whose hand brought us out.
20: 'I will stretch out my hand and strike Egypt with all my wonders' — the plagues are here announced as a unified divine action before any individual plague is described. The wonders (niflaot) will target Egypt's economic and ecological foundations: the Nile (blood), the land (frogs, gnats, flies), the livestock, the human body (boils), the sky (hail, darkness), and finally the firstborn. Each plague is calibrated to demonstrate YHWH's sovereignty over the specific domains Egypt's gods claimed to control (Hapy the Nile god, Khepri the beetle/frog god, Ra the sun god, etc.).
21: The Egyptians will give Israel 'favor' (chen) in their sight — the word chen (grace/favor) is the same used when Joseph found favor with Potiphar and the jailer (Genesis 39:4, 21). YHWH will cause the Egyptians to look favorably on the Israelites — a reversal of the contempt that fueled the oppression. The requesting of silver, gold, and clothing from Egyptian neighbors (Exodus 3:22) is the mechanism of this favor.
22: Every Israelite woman will ask her Egyptian neighbor for silver and gold jewelry and clothing — and will put them on her sons and daughters. 'You will plunder Egypt (ventsaltem et-Mitzraim).' The vocabulary is that of military plunder: the defeated city is stripped of its valuables by the victors. But Israel leaves not by military conquest but by divine decree, and the 'plunder' is the unpaid wages of centuries of forced labor. This motif was anticipated in the Abrahamic covenant (Genesis 15:14: 'afterward they will come out with great possessions'), and its fulfillment in Ex Exodus 12:35-36 is the closing of a promise made four centuries earlier. The centuries of unpaid labor are settled in a single night.
